access.entityLabel: Cambridge University Library MS Mm.IV.42

access.accessMethod: Submit the online imaging and permissions form and request the whole physical manuscript: both boards and inside boards, spine, head/tail/fore edges, pastedowns, all flyleaves and blanks, and every recto and verso of ff. 1-48; identify ff. 2r-48v and Dicta from f. 19r only as the work mapping within that full object
access.entityLabel: Cambridge University Library MS Mm.IV.42
access.format: Default 8-bit uncompressed JPEG, 600 ppi at 1:1 scale, RGB Adobe 1998; TIFF on request
notes: The published 21-999-image rate is GBP 7.50 per image before VAT; approximately 100 or more images would therefore likely cost at least about GBP 750 before extra surfaces, VAT, specialist handling, or licensing, but only CUL can provide an item-specific quote.
Ask first whether existing B971/Camb 30/5 microfilm or any complete internal photography can be supplied more economically.
Image supply does not confer publication permission; public Git hosting or commercial downstream use requires a specific written licence.

rights.basis: CUL's current terms reserve supplied copies for research/evaluation and require a specific written licence for reproduction, including academic, personal, nonprofit, or commercial publication.
rights.component: any CUL-supplied photography, scan, or microfilm copy
notes: A paid facsimile may be studied privately, but public Git hosting or reuse in commercial/POD projects is not authorized by purchase alone.

dateStatements.label: work and manuscript production
dateStatements.value: most likely April 1461 to the second half of 1462

entities.description: Ashby's parchment holograph of the incomplete Active Policy and Dicta work. The extant object has six complete quires of eight leaves; f. 1 is blank, the Latin preface begins at f. 2r, Dicta begins at f. 19r, and the text breaks off on f. 48v. At least one final quire is missing.

holding.notes: The current CUL search guide says most medieval manuscripts remain cataloged only in the five-volume nineteenth-century catalog; no exact modern public item record was found.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Mm.IV.42

physicalDescription.dimensions: approximately 270 x 180 mm; written space approximately 190 x 100 mm
physicalDescription.extent: 48 leaves plus two binding leaves; collation 1-6^8; at least one final quire missing
physicalDescription.format: Single columns averaging 28 lines or four rhyme-royal stanzas per page; Signet Secretary hand
notes: Quarter-bound twentieth-century binding by Douglas Cockerell & Son, December 1968, with leather spine and marbled-paper boards.
No completed decoration or rubrication; spaces for initials occur on ff. 2v, 4r, 5r, 6v, 9v, and 19r.
The first text page f. 2r is badly damaged and the final page f. 48v is worn.
physicalDescription.support: Parchment, with one twentieth-century rear paper flyleaf

rights.basis: CUL's current terms reserve supplied copies for research/evaluation and require a specific written licence for reproduction, including academic, personal, nonprofit, or commercial publication.

entities.description: The current scholarly argument identifying Mm.IV.42 as Ashby's holograph, dating Active Policy and Dicta to 1461-62, and refining the prison poem's date.

entities.description: A source study identifying the Liber as the basis of nearly all Dicta and eight passages of Active Policy, while distinguishing other proverbial sources.

next_steps: Submit the exact whole-object request through the CUL imaging form, with ff. 2r-48v and Dicta from f. 19r identified only as the work mapping.
Ask for the complete image inventory and an explicit list of any surfaces that cannot be photographed.
Ask separately for private-study supply terms, public Git hosting permission, and commercial/POD reuse terms.
notes: No repository enquiry has yet been sent.
openQuestions.question: Can CUL supply a complete digital reproduction of Mm.IV.42 covering every leaf side, blanks, flyleaves, pastedowns, both boards, spine, all three edges, and any stubs, and can an existing internal or microfilm surrogate reduce the cost?

next_steps: Ask Library of Congress reference staff for reel/frame metadata and reproduction options.
Ask CUL whether it retains or can scan the original or a duplicate microfilm for GBP 90 per reel under the current rate card.
Audit the Gale subscription item through an institution or trial before asserting complete coverage.
openQuestions.question: Does British Manuscripts Project B971 / Camb 30/5 contain the entire extant Mm.IV.42 object, and can its frames be obtained through the Library of Congress, CUL, or Gale with usable terms?

next_steps: Ask the Wren Library for an item-specific interpretation and quote only if physical binding documentation or noncommercial publication is needed.
Do not redistribute the cached bundle in a commercially enabled repository without written permission.
openQuestions.question: Can Trinity provide the physical spine and head, tail, and fore-edge views absent from the otherwise complete 522-canvas R.3.19 IIIF object, and how does its publication-permission policy interact with the exact CC BY-NC 4.0 licence?

next_steps: Compare Bateson's print systematically with the complete R.3.19 IIIF images and any future Mm.IV.42 facsimile.
Consult EETS correspondence or archival papers if exact intervention categories are important.
Treat italics contextually because they mark both expansions and ordinary emphasis or headings.
openQuestions.question: Did Bateson publish a fuller statement of her expansion, punctuation, capitalization, and emendation conventions than the brief acknowledgements and local notes in the 1899 volume?

relations.subjectLabel: British Manuscripts Project microfilm of CUL Mm.IV.42
Aline Douma's 2023 Active Policy and Dicta edition
Scattergood, The Date and Composition of George Ashby's Poems
Sobecki, The Signet Self: George Ashby's Autograph Writing
Bühler, The Liber de Dictis Philosophorum Antiquorum and Common Proverbs in George Ashby's Poems

reviewNotes: Whole-object caching is used throughout: the Trinity bundle contains all 522 canvases and the complete Bateson scan includes covers, preliminaries, all 105 printed pages, blanks, and binding views exposed by the provider.

The work portion is a mapping inside each complete object, never a cropped substitute: Trinity R.3.19 ff. 41r-45v is canvases 87-96; Mm.IV.42 ff. 2r-48v contains the incomplete Active Policy and Dicta work, with Dicta beginning at f. 19r.
Complete means all provider-exposed canvases or pages. Trinity does not expose separate spine, head, tail, or fore-edge photographs, so the IIIF object is not a complete physical-surface study.

The Harvard/Google Bateson derivative is partial because printed pp. 1-3 are absent. Its PDF, OCR text, page XML, and scan data are retained because they remain useful independent derivatives; the Stanford/ARLIMA PDF supplies a complete volume scan.
Underlying medieval texts, Bateson's editorial text, modern scholarship, page-image derivatives, and provider contract or licensing terms are modeled as separate rights layers.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is copied from University of Michigan CME item CME00057 and selectively encodes Mary Bateson's 1899 EETS edition George Ashby's Poems: the edition title page and the three printed text divisions A Prisoner's Reflections, Active Policy of a Prince, and Dicta et Opiniones Diversorum Philosophorum on printed pp. 1-100. It omits Bateson's introduction, contents, and Furnivall's List of Words. Bateson transcribed the prison poem from Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19, ff. 41r-45v, and the other two printed divisions from Cambridge University Library MS Mm.IV.42, ff. 2r-48v. Current scholarship identifies Mm.IV.42 as Ashby's holograph and reads Active Policy and Dicta as two sections of one incomplete work, probably composed in 1461-62. Trinity R.3.19 is publicly digitized: the complete 522-canvas provider object is cached at 1600-pixel width, with the poem mapped to canvases 87-96. No open complete color facsimile of Mm.IV.42 was found. A legacy microfilm exists as British Manuscripts Project B971, Camb 30/5, and CUL accepts paid full-object imaging requests. Complete provider objects and exact links are cached for one full Bateson volume, one incomplete alternate scan, the Trinity IIIF object, CUL's printed catalog volume, two modern studies, and three access or publication inventories.

catalogSummary.author: George Ashby

catalogSummary.title: George Ashby's Poems

catalogingSubject.label: George Ashby's Poems
catalogingSubject.scope_note: An encoding-specific collection record for the two extant works by George Ashby represented in CME00057: A Prisoner's Reflections and the incomplete Active Policy of a Prince with its Dicta et Opiniones Diversorum Philosophorum section. Mary Bateson's 1899 edition and the CME XML number Active Policy and Dicta as two printed poems, but current scholarship and the holograph's layout support treating them as two sections of one work. The collection title is editorial; this record does not collapse the medieval works, two physical manuscripts, Bateson's edition, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML into one object.

contentParts.label: A Prisoner's Reflections
notes: Bateson's a/b manuscript references correspond to recto/verso in the current Trinity catalog and IIIF labels.
contentParts.summary: Ashby's autobiographical prison complaint and counsel on sustaining patience in adversity, followed by an envoy and explicit conclusion.

contentParts.label: Active Policy of a Prince, including Dicta et Opiniones Diversorum Philosophorum
notes: The work is textually incomplete: the extant holograph ends midway through the 312th stanza and has lost at least one final quire.
contentParts.summary: An incomplete mirror for Prince Edward of Westminster: a Latin authorial preface, English prologue, political advice organized around past, present, and future, and an extensive Dicta section of Latin authorities with Middle English verse renderings.

contentParts.label: De activa pollecia principis
contentParts.summary: Latin prose framing, twelve-stanza poetic prologue, introductory advice, and sections In tempore preterito, Iam de tempore presenti, and Iam de tempore futuro.

contentParts.label: Dicta et Opiniones Diversorum Philosophorum
notes: Item count follows the 181 numbered rhyme-royal groups in the encoding, not discrete underlying philosophical works.
contentParts.summary: A disjointed, incomplete sequence of Latin moral sayings attributed to philosophers, each normally followed by one or occasionally two English rhyme-royal stanzas, continuing the advice to Prince Edward.

assertion.notes: The poem says it was written in prison in 1463; Sobecki narrows the interval using the Michaelmas commitment and the medieval calendar.

dateStatements.display: Between Michaelmas 1463 and 24 March 1464 in modern reckoning; self-dated 1463

assertion.notes: Current preferred scholarly window based on Ashby's reference to himself as lately Margaret's Signet clerk, the holograph's unfinished state, and newly assembled life records; earlier scholarship proposed 1460-61, circa 1463, 1468, or 1470.

dateStatements.display: Most likely between April 1461 and the second half of 1462

assertion.notes: This dates the editorial collection represented by CME00057, not necessarily the first separate printing of every component poem.

dateStatements.display: Published as George Ashby's Poems by Mary Bateson in 1899

extent.description: Two abstract works represented as three numbered divisions in Bateson's edition and CME XML. A Prisoner's Reflections has 50 verse groups and 350 lines. The incomplete Active Policy and Dicta work has 312 verse groups and 2,181 Middle English verse lines: 131 groups/918 lines in the Active Policy division and 181 groups/1,263 lines in Dicta, plus integral Latin prose. Counts describe the encoding, not the amount lost after Mm.IV.42 f. 48v.

formStatements.description: The collection is predominantly Middle English rhyme-royal verse, but Active Policy and Dicta include an integral Latin prose preface and repeated prose Latin maxims; the work-level collection is therefore mixed rather than pure verse.

scope.description: Authorial prose preface, section headings, and extensive philosophers' sayings preceding English verse renderings

notes: The collection title and three numbered printed divisions are edition-level facts; the two-work catalog model reflects the physical manuscripts and current scholarship.
The self-date 1463 for A Prisoner's Reflections and the modern 1463-64 interval are compatible under medieval English year reckoning and the poem's Michaelmas chronology.
Mixed form records integral Latin prose, not merely editorial notes: Mm.IV.42 begins with an authorial Latin preface and Dicta alternates Latin prose sayings with Middle English verse.
The 2,531-line extent is the encoded surviving text and does not estimate the missing end of Dicta.
Mary Bateson is displayed as editor because CME00057 represents her 1899 edition; medieval authorship and compilation remain George Ashby's.

next_steps: Follow Aline Douma's forthcoming TEAMS edition and monograph for any revised dating based on newly identified records or manuscript evidence.
Keep earlier 1460-61, circa-1463, 1468, and 1470 proposals in the research history rather than presenting them as coequal current dates.
openQuestions.question: Can the likely April 1461-second-half-1462 composition window for Active Policy and Dicta be narrowed further?

next_steps: Do not infer a missing-line count from the one known lost quire because the extant source sequence is not systematic and the work may never have been completed.
Compare the philosophical source sequence and any future material analysis of the codex only as qualified evidence.
openQuestions.question: How much of the Dicta section and any intended conclusion was lost after Mm.IV.42 f. 48v?

next_steps: Treat Bühler's relationship to Paris BnF lat. 6652 as a textual-affinity hypothesis, not identification of Ashby's physical exemplar.
Review current stemmatic work before adding a manuscript-level source witness.
openQuestions.question: Which physical copy or textual branch of the Liber Philosophorum Moralium Antiquorum did Ashby use?

assertion.notes: The current evidence supports composition in England after the Lancastrian royal family's 1461 exile, but does not securely locate Ashby in a particular town.

scope.description: Lancastrian and Yorkist political conflict, imprisonment, and advice for the English crown prince
Compilation and extraction of political and moral advice, including the Latin philosophers' sayings
Middle English rhyme-royal translations or verse renderings following Latin sayings
Immediate 1899 printed representation, not medieval authorship or compilation

subjects.label: Royal service and the Signet Office
Lancastrian politics and the Wars of the Roses

summaries.value: Two surviving works by the Lancastrian Signet clerk George Ashby. A Prisoner's Reflections combines autobiographical complaint about wrongful imprisonment in the Fleet with moral and devotional counsel on patience in adversity. Active Policy of a Prince, written for Edward of Westminster, advises a future king about memory, present government, future policy, royal authority, ministers, finance, rebellion, war, commerce, and public order; its Dicta section extends that teaching through Latin sayings attributed to ancient philosophers followed by Middle English rhyme-royal renderings.

CME00057 follows Bateson's three printed divisions, but the manuscript structure and current scholarship support a two-work model: the Trinity prison poem and one incomplete Mm.IV.42 work comprising Active Policy and Dicta. The encoding contains 2,531 Middle English verse lines in 362 LG groups, along with substantial embedded Latin prose. Mm.IV.42 breaks off during Dicta and has lost at least one final quire.

notes: An editorial collection title rather than a known fifteenth-century title for a single work or medieval compilation.
Modern conventional title; Trinity's catalog labels the item Complaint of a Prisoner.

titles.value: At the ende of Somer, when wynter began

notes: Used here for the whole incomplete work, including the Dicta section, following current scholarship.
A manuscript section heading, not evidence that the section is an independent work.

work.summary: Two surviving works by the Lancastrian Signet clerk George Ashby. A Prisoner's Reflections combines autobiographical complaint about wrongful imprisonment in the Fleet with moral and devotional counsel on patience in adversity. Active Policy of a Prince, written for Edward of Westminster, advises a future king about memory, present government, future policy, royal authority, ministers, finance, rebellion, war, commerce, and public order; its Dicta section extends that teaching through Latin sayings attributed to ancient philosophers followed by Middle English rhyme-royal renderings.
